claims.text: The Mary Celeste was found drifting and abandoned on December 4, 1872, with all 10 people aboard — Captain Briggs, his wife Sarah, daughter Sophia, and seven crew — missing.

evidence.quote:
asn't a
soul in sight. The ship's name was
painted on her home. Mary Celeste, an
American vessel that left New York weeks
earlier. Morehouse recognized the name.
He knew her captai

claims.rationale: The Mary Celeste is one of the best-documented maritime mysteries. The December 1872 abandonment, Captain Briggs's family, and crew of seven are all well-established historical facts in Cobb-Briggs family records and the subsequent Gibraltar salvage hearing.

claims.text: The Mary Celeste was in good condition when found — meals on the table, personal belongings undisturbed, cargo of 1,700 barrels of industrial alcohol intact — ruling out pirates as the explanation.

evidence.quote:
more they looked, the more
uneasy they
became. The galley showed signs of
recent use. A meal was still on the
table, halfeaten. There's plenty of foo

claims.rationale: The Mary Celeste's cargo of ~1,700 barrels of industrial alcohol, the intact state, and untouched personal items are all well-documented from the Gibraltar Court of Inquiry testimony. The absence of pirates is widely accepted by historians.

dependencies.rationale: Details what was found aboard the abandoned Mary Celeste.

claims.text: The most likely explanation for the Mary Celeste is that fumes from leaking industrial alcohol led Briggs to order evacuation into a lifeboat that was then lost in a storm.

evidence.quote:
most likely explanation is
the cargo, industrial alcohol. There
were a few empty barrels. Alcohol might
have leaked into the BGE, fumes rising,
and the crew could sm

claims.rationale: The alcohol-fume-panic hypothesis is one of the leading mainstream theories, advanced by historians Charles Fay and Brian Hicks. Hicks argued empty barrels (9 of the 1,701) suggest leakage. Still a theory, not proven — host qualifies it as 'most likely' and 'mystery remains'.

dependencies.rationale: Offers a mundane explanation for the disappearance.

claims.text: The Ourang Medan ghost-ship story — a 1940s radio distress call from a Dutch freighter whose crew was found dead with expressions of terror — may be fabricated, as many historians doubt the ship ever existed.

evidence.quote:
Orangadan is different. Many
historians doubt it existed. No official
records confirm this Dutch freighter.
But there is a rumor that the ship was
carrying chemica

claims.rationale: The Ourang Medan was never registered in any Lloyd's of London shipping registry, no Dutch shipping record survives, and the distress-call story first appeared in an obscure 1948 Dutch-Indonesian newspaper with vague provenance. Host's skeptical framing is correct — consensus among maritime historians is the story is probably apocryphal or heavily embellished.

claims.text: The Carroll A. Deering was a real 5-masted schooner that ran aground abandoned at Cape Hatteras on January 31, 1921, with a meal laid out in the galley and no crew.

evidence.quote:
ary 31st, 1921, CP Brady was
on lookout duty for the Coast Guard at
Cape Hatteris. Wait, who had her ass?
Cape Hatteris in North Carolina. Ah,
that makes more s

claims.rationale: Carroll A. Deering grounding on Diamond Shoals is well-documented; US Navy, FBI, State Department, and Commerce Department all investigated. Ship, crew complement of 10, and abandonment are established historical facts.

claims.text: The most likely explanation for the Carroll A. Deering is mutiny — witnesses reported hearing the crew shouting about overthrowing the captain before the ship grounded.

evidence.quote:
most likely answer. Witnesses reported
hearing the crew shouting about
overthrowing the captain days before the
ship was found. The crew pr

claims.rationale: Mutiny is one of several competing hypotheses (others: rum-running, Bolshevik piracy, storm). Pre-grounding shouting-about-mutiny testimony is in the official records but is disputed. The case remains officially unsolved; host's 'most likely' framing is a lean, not a consensus.

dependencies.rationale: Offers a candidate explanation for the grounding.

claims.text: The SS Baychimo was abandoned in Arctic ice in 1931 and continued to drift unmanned for decades, with sporadic sightings as late as 1969 (and some Inuit claims into 2006).

evidence.quote:
ty. The SS
Bachimo got stuck in ice near Alaska in
1931. The ice was crushing the hull, so
the crew abandoned ship. They assumed
the ship was doomed. It wasn't.

claims.rationale: The SS Baychimo's decades-long unmanned drift through Arctic waters is well-documented — Hudson's Bay Company cargo ship, 1931 abandonment, last confirmed sighting 1969. The story is a real phenomenon (reinforced hull, Arctic currents) and is not supernatural, as host correctly clarifies.

claims.text: The 1968 Alfasto (Alefoz) disappearances — a fishing boat whose crew refused rescue and later vanished, with one mummified corpse and a torn-out notebook — suggest the crew was engaged in drug trafficking or other illegal activity.

evidence.quote:
ship is more legend than
reality, but it's not impossible. The
Salt and Basin has changed dramatically
over centuries.
It was once Lake Kawa, an ancient inland
sea about

claims.rationale: The 1968 Alfasto / Joyita-era Canary Islands incident has factual core (disappearance, later discovery with corpse), but fine details like the mummified corpse position and torn notebook pages vary across retellings. Drug-smuggling is a reasonable hypothesis but unproven. Host marks as best-guess speculation.

claims.text: The Bermuda Triangle was the uncharted Atlantic region where Captain Griffin's Ellen Austin salvage attempts in 1881 famously failed — long before the region had that name.

evidence.quote:
now it as the
Bermuda Triangle.
A really weird ghost ship story is the
Alausto. This fishing boat disappeared
in 19

claims.rationale: The Ellen Austin salvage legend is part of Bermuda Triangle folklore but has poor historical documentation. Lloyd's records do not confirm the crew disappearance and re-disappearance story; the Bermuda Triangle framing was coined by Vincent Gaddis in 1964 and popularized by Charles Berlitz. Mostly retro-labeled legend.

claims.text: The 1870 San Bernardino Guardian report of a Spanish galleon buried in the Mojave Desert inspired a series of subsequent 'lost ship of the desert' claims.

evidence.quote:
rdino
Guardian reported a ship discovered deep
in the Mojave Desert. A Spanish gallion
at least 250 ft long, completely intact.
The desert winds had s

claims.rationale: The San Bernardino Guardian did publish the galleon-in-desert story in the 1870s, and multiple subsequent reports (1892, 1907, 1949) are matters of newspaper record. The reports themselves are real; whether the ship exists is a separate question.

claims.text: A Spanish galleon really could have sailed inland via the Colorado River during a 1600s flood and become buried by sand as the Salton Basin dried — geologically plausible.

evidence.quote:
rms the basin's water did
fluctuate, and Spanish records document
pearl hunting expeditions in this
region. So, while treasure hunters may
have embell

claims.rationale: The Salton Basin (Lake Cahuilla) did historically fluctuate with Colorado River floods, and Juan de Iturbe did lead pearl-hunting expeditions in the Gulf of California in 1612-1615. The 'ship floats inland and gets buried' scenario is geologically possible but unproven — no modern radar/GPR survey has ever located such a ship.

dependencies.rationale: Provides a plausible mechanism for the 1870+ reports.

claims.text: Modern ghost ship cases typically have straightforward explanations — the High Aim 6 involved murder by a crewmember, the Kaz II had a man-overboard, and Tai Ching 21 likely met pirates.

evidence.quote:
bout our relationship with
the sea. Despite our technology and
understanding, the ocean is still vast,
unpredictable, and sometimes deadly. The
sea has claimed thousands

claims.rationale: High Aim 6 (2003): crew ultimately confessed under Taiwanese investigation — mutiny-related. Kaz II (2007): investigators concluded the three men went overboard in stages, likely while dealing with a fishing line. Tai Ching 21 (2008): exact cause officially undetermined but piracy is a mainstream candidate. Host's mundane-explanation framing is largely correct; individual details vary.
